title: The real cost of hiring vs. building an AI employee
excerpt: Salary is the number everyone focuses on. It's also the smallest part of the total cost. Here's what a hire actually costs when you add everything up.
author: Simon Insinger
content:
## Salary is just the start

When a founder says "I need to hire for this role," they're thinking about the salary number. That's usually the least interesting part of the cost.

Here's what actually goes into a full-time hire:

- Recruiting: job postings, recruiter fees (15-25% of salary), and the hours you spend interviewing
- Onboarding: 30-90 days before they're actually productive
- Benefits: health insurance, 401k, PTO — typically 25-40% on top of salary
- Your time: managing, reviewing, giving feedback, having 1-on-1s
- Turnover: average tenure is 2-3 years, then you do it all again

## Running the numbers

For a mid-level operations role:

| Cost | Traditional Hire | AI Employee |
|---|---|---|
| Base salary | $65,000/year | — |
| Benefits (30%) | $19,500/year | — |
| Recruiting | $10,000 (one-time) | — |
| Build cost | — | $5,000-12,000 (one-time) |
| Ongoing cost | $84,500/year | $250-500/month |
| Ramp time | 60-90 days | 2 weeks |
| Availability | 40 hrs/week | 24/7 |

Over three years with no raises and no turnover (optimistic), a traditional hire costs about $263,500. An AI employee with ongoing support maxes out around $30,000. And it won't leave for a competitor.

## Beyond the spreadsheet

The cost case is clear, but there are other things worth considering.

Speed. An AI employee is ready in 2 weeks instead of 2-4 months.

Consistency. No bad days, no ramp-up dips, no personality clashes.

Scalability. Need another one? Duplicate and customize. No job posting required.

## Where hiring still makes sense

Not everything should be automated. Roles that require human judgment, creative problem-solving, or building real relationships with people — those still need humans.

But for work that's primarily operational — data processing, scheduling, outreach, research, reporting — the math is hard to argue with.

## A simple test

Three questions about any role:

1. Is the work mostly pattern-based? If yes, AI can handle it.
2. Does it require building human relationships? If no, AI works.
3. Would 24/7 availability help? If yes, AI is the obvious choice.

If your answers are yes, no, yes — that role is a strong candidate.
